title: Build Understandable Option Groups
summary: Build and verify form grouping options in transport and accessibility preference form through prediction, inspection, explanation, cumulative code, and changed-case transfer.
objectives: Explain form grouping options from purpose, behavior, and evidence.
Apply form grouping options without discarding earlier valid work.
Diagnose the named misconception in changed content, input, and failure states.

steps.title: 01 · Predict the transport and accessibility preference form case · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 1: Which claim preserves form grouping options when data, input, and layout change? Scenario: transport and accessibility preference form.
steps.why: Build Understandable Option Groups makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 1 in transport and accessibility preference form.

content.title: transport and accessibility preference form evidence
content.text: For Build Understandable Option Groups, choose the form grouping options claim that preserves the transport and accessibility preference form purpose, native behavior, and understandable relationships when presentation changes.

options.text: Transport and accessibility preference form case 1: visually adjacent radio buttons automatically form an announced group.
Transport and accessibility preference form case 1: choose radio, checkbox, or select from the choice relationship and expose group purpose with native structure.
Transport and accessibility preference form case 1: give each radio a different name so the browser knows each label is unique.

steps.title: 02 · Read the transport and accessibility preference form case · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 2: Which claim preserves form grouping options when data, input, and layout change? Scenario: transport and accessibility preference form.
steps.why: Build Understandable Option Groups makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 2 in transport and accessibility preference form.

content.text: Fieldset and legend give a related control group an announced purpose. Radio controls sharing one name represent one mutually exclusive choice. Checkboxes represent independent yes-or-no choices. Select is useful for a compact bounded list, but native radios may make a small critical choice easier to compare. Visual proximity alone does not create programmatic grouping. Apply it to transport and accessibility preference form.
